title: Red Wine on Cotton
stainChemistry: Red wine contains anthocyanin pigments — water-soluble tannins that bond to cotton cellulose fibers through hydrogen bonding. Cotton's porous fiber structure absorbs liquid quickly. The acidic pH (3–4) of wine enhances pigment-fiber bonding. Heat or agitation accelerates penetration and oxidation, shifting the stain from bright red to brown and making removal harder over time.
whyThisWorks: Tannin-specific spotters break the hydrogen bonds between wine pigment and cotton cellulose. Cold water flushes the broken pigment out before it can re-bond. Oxidizing agents (H₂O₂) cleave the chromophore double bonds that give wine its color. Cotton tolerates this chemistry well — no fiber damage risk when using agents at appropriate concentrations.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Blot immediately with a clean white cloth — do not rub. Work from the outer edge inward to prevent spreading. Flush the back of the fabric with cold water to push the stain out through the fiber rather than deeper in.
Apply a small amount of neutral liquid dish soap directly to the stain. Work it in gently with your fingertip. Let it dwell 3–5 minutes, then blot with a clean damp cloth.
Mix 1 tablespoon white vinegar with 1 cup cold water. Apply to the stain and let it dwell 2–3 minutes. The mild acid helps release tannin bonds from the cotton fiber. Blot thoroughly.

For stubborn or dried stains, apply 3% hydrogen peroxide (drugstore variety) directly to the remaining stain. Let it dwell 5–10 minutes. The oxidizer breaks the chromophore bonds that give wine its color. Blot and rinse with cold water. Test on a hidden area first — check for colorfastness before applying to the stain.
Flush thoroughly with cold water to remove all cleaning agents. Check the stain before machine washing — heat from the dryer will permanently set any remaining pigment.

homeSolutions: For a fresh red wine stain on cotton: blot immediately, then pour a small amount of club soda to help lift the pigment while it's still wet. Follow with cold water flush and dish soap. The faster you act, the better — wine sets into cotton within minutes.

For dried or set red wine: soak the stained area in a mixture of 1 tablespoon dish soap + 1 tablespoon white vinegar + 2 cups cold water for 30 minutes before treating. OxiClean or similar oxygen-based stain removers work well on set tannin stains in cotton — follow package instructions and check for colorfastness first.
materialWarnings: Never use hot water — heat permanently sets tannin stains into cotton fiber.
Never put in the dryer until the stain is fully removed — dryer heat locks the stain in permanently.
Never rub — rubbing spreads the stain and drives pigment deeper into the fabric.
Test hydrogen peroxide on a hidden seam first — white and light cotton is generally safe, but colored cotton may fade.

professional.name: Tannin (acidic tannin spotter)
professional.note: Apply and dwell 5–10 minutes before flushing

professional.name: H₂O₂ 6%
professional.use: Oxidizer for stubborn or set stains
professional.note: Professional concentration — test first, do not leave on colored cotton more than 5 minutes

consumer.name: OxiClean Versatile Stain Remover
consumer.note: Follow package directions — effective on dried tannin in cotton

consumer.name: Wine Away Red Wine Stain Remover
consumer.note: Apply immediately while stain is wet for best results

escalation.when: If the stain survives cold water, dish soap, vinegar, and hydrogen peroxide, the tannins have cross-linked into the cotton fiber. Consider professional cleaning.
escalation.whatToTell: Red wine stain on cotton, treated with cold water and dish soap at home. May have been partially set by heat or time.
escalation.specialistType: dry cleaner or textile care specialist
